perturbation.original_text: Party B confirms the share of revenues of the preceding month payable to Party A on the first day of each calendar month (hereinafter referred to as (the "Reference Date," and postponed in the event of public holidays) according to the above Article 5.2.2. Upon the confirmation of the share of revenues of such month, it shall not be adjusted, unless there is sufficient proof evidencing the statistics is not correct.
perturbation.changed_text: Party B confirms the share of revenues of the preceding month payable to Party A on the 30th day of the subsequent month (hereinafter referred to as (the "Reference Date,") according to the above Article 5.2.2. Upon the confirmation of the share of revenues of such month, it shall not be adjusted, unless there is sufficient proof evidencing the statistics is not correct.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the revenue confirmation to the 30th day of the subsequent month creates a delay that may violate prompt payment regulations depending on the nature of the services and jurisdiction. If this falls under services performed, this is a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 2751 requires a written contract for certain service arrangements. Delaying revenue confirmation may violate prompt payment aspects implied in service contracts, especially if payment is tied to this confirmation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2751 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. The Contract of Employment [2750 - 2755] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 2751. (a) Whenever an employer enters into a contract of employment with an employee for services to be rendered within this state and the contemplated method of payment of the employee involves commissions, the contract shall be in writing and shall set forth the method by which the commissions shall be computed and paid. (b) The employer shall give a signed copy of the contract to every employee who is a party thereto and shall obtain a signed receipt for the contract from each employee. In the case of a contract that expires and where the parties nevertheless continue to work under the terms of the expired contract, the contract terms are presumed to remain in full force and effect until the contract is superseded or employment is terminated by either party. (c) As used in this section, “commissions” has the meaning set forth in Section 204.1. For purposes of this section only, “commission” does not include any of the following: (1) Short-term productivity bonuses
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a significant delay in revenue confirmation, shifting it from the 'first day of each calendar month' to the '30th day of the subsequent month.' The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight that this delay may contradict prompt payment regulations, especially in service contracts. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Labor Code § 2751(a), requires written contracts for services involving commissions and specifies that the contract should detail how commissions are computed and paid. While the snippet doesn't explicitly mention payment deadlines, delaying revenue confirmation directly impacts the 'method' of commission payment, creating an inconsistency. The altered timeline undermines the implicit expectation of timely payment associated with service contracts governed by this section, thus creating a contradiction under the Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction definition, as the altered deadline violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: Party A provides the invoice of the share of revenues in the preceding month within the first 5 working days in each month in advance, Party B warrants to pay the share of revenues to Party A prior to the 20t h day of each month (postponed in the event of public holidays) according to Article 4.1 hereof. In the event that Party A objects the payment from Party B, it shall propose within the first 5 working days in each month in writing, or it will be deemed as no objection. Party B shall review within three working days upon receipt of Party A's written objection and notify the results thereof to Party A.
perturbation.changed_text: Party A provides the invoice of the share of revenues in the preceding month within the first 15 working days in each month, Party B warrants to pay the share of revenues to Party A prior to the 60th day of each month according to Article 4.1 hereof. In the event that Party A objects the payment from Party B, it shall propose within the first 10 working days in each month in writing, or it will be deemed as no objection. Party B shall review within ten working days upon receipt of Party A's written objection and notify the results thereof to Party A.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the payment deadline to the 60th day of the month may violate prompt payment laws, especially if services are involved. The extended period for objection and review exacerbates the delay.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Migrant and Seasonal Agricultural Worker Protection Act mandates timely payment of wages, and a 60-day delay could be considered a violation depending on the nature of Party A's services and whether they qualify under this act. The ambiguity regarding 'services' here is exploited.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract extends the payment deadline to the 60th day of the month, and extends the period for objection and review. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate prompt payment laws, particularly the Migrant and Seasonal Agricultural Worker Protection Act, depending on the services provided by Party A. The original text provides a 20-day payment window; the modified text extends this period significantly, undermining the principle of timely payment, which could be a violation depending on applicable laws regarding prompt payment for services, as suggested by the Law Explanation. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition states that inconsistencies arise when 'time-sensitive obligations in a contract do not align with legal requirements' and 'a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.' The change significantly extends payment timelines, which potentially violates the 'Migrant and Seasonal Agricultural Worker Protection Act' detailed in both snippets, thus contradicting legal standards.

perturbation.original_text: Within fifteen (15) days upon the termination of this Agreement, the parties shall delete the links between their websites.
perturbation.changed_text: Within ninety (90) days upon the termination of this Agreement, the parties shall delete the links between their websites.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the deadline for deleting website links to 90 days introduces potential legal conflicts. While this might seem minor, if the continued presence of links creates trademark or copyright infringement issues, a 90-day delay could be unreasonable and violate intellectual property laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 addresses trademark infringement. If the continued display of links after termination implies endorsement or affiliation when none exists, and this causes consumer confusion, the 90-day delay could violate trademark law by prolonging the infringing activity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the deadline for deleting website links from 15 to 90 days, creating a potential conflict with trademark law. According to 15 U.S. Code § 1114, the use of a registered mark in commerce that is likely to cause confusion can lead to civil action. The Perturbation Explanation posits that the delay in removing links could cause consumer confusion. Therefore, prolonging the presence of potentially infringing links for 90 days could violate trademark law.
